subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Priya seeks an interlocutory injunction to prevent a competitor from using her confidential client list pending the outcome of the trial. The motion is heard by a judge in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice.
call_of_question: What three-part test must Priya satisfy to obtain the interlocutory injunction?

options.A: She must prove on a balance of probabilities that she will succeed at trial.
options.B: She must satisfy the three-part test from RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG): (1) serious question to be tried, (2) irreparable harm if the injunction is refused, and (3) the balance of convenience favours granting the injunction.
options.C: She must prove her right to the relief claimed beyond a reasonable doubt.
options.D: She must establish a prima facie case for the relief claimed and demonstrate a preponderance of evidence in her favour.

explanation: The governing test for an interlocutory injunction in Ontario is the three-part test established by the Supreme Court of Canada in RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311: (1) Is there a serious question to be tried? (2) Will the moving party suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is refused? (3) Does the balance of convenience favour granting or refusing the injunction?
why_A_wrong: The interlocutory injunction test does not require proof on a balance of probabilities that the moving party will succeed at trial. The 'serious question' branch is a deliberately low threshold.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The three-part RJR-MacDonald test is the applicable framework for interlocutory injunctions.
why_C_wrong: Beyond a reasonable doubt is the criminal standard of proof and has no application to civil interlocutory injunction motions.
why_D_wrong: Prima facie case and preponderance of evidence are not the correct formulation of the interlocutory injunction test under RJR-MacDonald.

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: On a motion for an interlocutory injunction, the defendant argues that the plaintiff must demonstrate it is more likely than not to succeed at trial in order to satisfy the first branch of the RJR-MacDonald test.
call_of_question: Which statement correctly describes the 'serious question to be tried' branch of the RJR-MacDonald test?

options.A: The plaintiff must show it is more likely than not to succeed at the trial of the action.
options.B: The plaintiff must show its claim is not frivolous or vexatious; this is a deliberately low threshold that does not require the court to assess the merits in any depth.
options.C: The plaintiff must establish a strong prima facie case, demonstrating that it would likely obtain a permanent injunction at trial.
options.D: The plaintiff must produce evidence that would be sufficient to convince a jury of the facts underlying the claim.

explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada in RJR-MacDonald confirmed that the 'serious question to be tried' is a low threshold. The moving party need only show that the claim is neither frivolous nor vexatious. The court is not to engage in an extensive review of the merits at this stage. A probability of success is not required. The strong prima facie case standard is reserved for mandatory injunctions and certain exceptional categories.
why_A_wrong: Probability of success is not required at the serious question stage. RJR-MacDonald sets a deliberately low threshold — the claim must merely not be frivolous or vexatious.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The serious question branch is a low threshold requiring only that the claim is not frivolous or vexatious.
why_C_wrong: A strong prima facie case is the higher threshold applied to mandatory injunctions, not the standard interlocutory (prohibitive) injunction test.
why_D_wrong: The serious question test does not require the kind of evidence needed to convince a jury. It is assessed on the motion record and applies a low threshold.

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Kwame seeks an interlocutory injunction to prevent a buyer from taking possession of his business, which he alleges was sold under duress. The defendant argues that any loss Kwame suffers before trial can be fully compensated by a damages award.
call_of_question: How does the 'irreparable harm' requirement of the RJR-MacDonald test apply in this situation?

options.A: Irreparable harm is satisfied by showing that any harm will occur before trial, regardless of whether damages would be adequate.
options.B: The court should grant the injunction to preserve the status quo regardless of whether a damages award would adequately compensate Kwame.
options.C: Irreparable harm exists only where the physical harm to the moving party is permanent and cannot be undone.
options.D: Kwame must show that the harm he will suffer cannot be adequately compensated by an award of damages at trial; if damages would be adequate, he does not satisfy this branch of the test.

explanation: The 'irreparable harm' branch of the RJR-MacDonald test requires the moving party to demonstrate that, if the injunction is refused, the harm suffered pending trial cannot be adequately remedied by damages. If a damages award at trial would fully compensate the moving party, the harm is not 'irreparable' and this branch is not satisfied. The court is not required to preserve the status quo in all cases; the adequacy of damages is the central question.
why_A_wrong: The mere fact that harm will occur before trial does not satisfy the irreparable harm branch. The critical question is whether damages would be an adequate remedy.
why_B_wrong: Courts do not grant injunctions solely to maintain the status quo. The moving party must satisfy all three RJR-MacDonald branches, including showing that damages are inadequate.
why_C_wrong: 'Irreparable harm' does not require physical permanence. It means that damages would not be an adequate remedy — for example, because loss of goodwill, confidential information, or unique business opportunities cannot be fully monetized.
why_D_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The irreparable harm branch asks whether damages are an adequate remedy; if they are, the branch is not satisfied.
tested_concepts: damages as adequate remedy defeats injunction

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: A defendant moves to discharge an interlocutory injunction previously granted ex parte. The plaintiff, a small technology vendor, established a serious question to be tried and that it would suffer irreparable harm if the injunction were refused (loss of its sole client contract). The defendant, a large publicly-traded corporation, argues that maintaining the injunction will delay a product launch by six weeks and cost it an estimated $4 million in lost revenue — a sum it quantifies precisely in affidavit evidence. The plaintiff offers an undertaking as to damages. The judge is now assessing the third branch of the RJR-MacDonald test.
call_of_question: Under the third branch of the RJR-MacDonald test, which of the following most accurately describes how the court should assess the balance of convenience in these circumstances?

options.A: The injunction must be discharged because the defendant has quantified its harm precisely — once a moving party establishes greater quantifiable harm than the plaintiff, the balance of convenience automatically favours discharge.
options.B: The injunction must be maintained because satisfying the first two RJR-MacDonald elements — serious question and irreparable harm — creates a presumption in favour of maintaining the status quo that cannot be displaced on the balance of convenience alone.
options.C: The court weighs the relative harm to each party from granting or refusing the injunction, considering all relevant factors including the undertaking as to damages, the capacity of each party to absorb harm, and the public interest; where the balance is genuinely equal, courts generally favour the status quo.
options.D: The court must discharge the injunction because the defendant's harm is quantifiable in money and therefore, by definition, compensable — once a party's alleged harm is monetizable, irreparable harm is negated and the injunction cannot stand.

explanation: Under the third branch of the RJR-MacDonald test (RJR-MacDonald Inc. v. Canada (A.G.), [1994] 1 SCR 311), the court weighs which party would suffer greater harm from the grant or refusal of the injunction. This involves considering: the nature and quantifiability of each party's harm, whether the plaintiff's undertaking as to damages adequately protects the defendant, the relative financial capacity of the parties to absorb temporary harm, the public interest, and any other relevant factors. The fact that the defendant has quantified its loss at $4 million is not automatically determinative — courts weigh all factors holistically. The plaintiff's undertaking as to damages is directly relevant to the defendant's quantified loss. Where the balance is genuinely equal, courts tend to favour preserving the status quo.
why_A_wrong: A states a rule that does not exist. The Quantifiability-Determines-Balance Trap: the ability to quantify harm precisely does not automatically tip the balance of convenience toward discharge. Under RJR-MacDonald, the court weighs all relevant factors holistically. A defendant who can precisely quantify its loss may actually be better protected by the plaintiff's undertaking as to damages — precise quantification does not eliminate judicial discretion or automatically favour one party.
why_B_wrong: B misstates the law. The First-Two-Elements Presumption Trap: satisfying the first two branches of RJR-MacDonald creates no irrebuttable or presumptive entitlement to the injunction. All three branches must be weighed, and a strong showing on the balance of convenience against the plaintiff can outweigh success on the first two elements. The balance of convenience is an independent and substantive inquiry.
why_C_wrong: C is correct. Under the third branch of RJR-MacDonald, the court weighs the harm to each party from granting or refusing the injunction, considering all relevant factors: the plaintiff's undertaking as to damages (which may compensate the defendant's $4 million quantified loss), the parties' relative capacity to absorb harm, the public interest, and whether the harm on each side is truly irreparable or compensable. Equal balance favours the status quo, but an unequal balance may tip either way depending on the facts.
why_D_wrong: D conflates the second branch (irreparable harm) with the third branch balance of convenience analysis. The Monetizable-Harm-Negates-Injunction Trap: whether harm is quantifiable in money is relevant to the second branch (irreparable harm, which the court has already found here), not a ground to defeat the injunction at the third branch. The undertaking as to damages addresses monetizable harm directly — the defendant's $4 million figure is an argument for adequacy of the undertaking, not an automatic basis for discharge.
tested_concepts: undertaking as to damages as a factor

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Amara seeks an ex parte interlocutory injunction — without prior notice to the other side — to freeze her former business partner's bank account. She believes the partner is about to transfer assets out of the jurisdiction.
call_of_question: What additional considerations apply when a moving party seeks an ex parte interlocutory injunction?

options.A: Ex parte injunctions are not available in commercial disputes; Amara must give notice to the other side.
options.B: On an ex parte motion, the moving party need only satisfy the regular RJR-MacDonald test because the other side can bring a motion to set aside the order if it disagrees.
options.C: On an ex parte motion, the moving party must demonstrate urgency making prior notice impracticable and must make full and frank disclosure of all material facts, including facts that may favour the other side.
options.D: Ex parte injunctions are permanent orders unless the opposing party obtains a court order to vary or set them aside.

explanation: Under RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311, Ex parte injunctions are exceptional because they are granted without hearing from the opposing party. Two additional duties attach beyond the standard RJR-MacDonald test. First, the moving party must demonstrate urgency sufficient to justify dispensing with notice — typically that giving notice would cause irreparable harm (e.g., dissipation of assets). Second, and critically, the moving party owes a duty of full and frank disclosure to the court: it must disclose all material facts, including those that favour the opposing party, because the court cannot hear the other side. Breach of this duty can result in the injunction being set aside.
why_A_wrong: Ex parte injunctions are available in commercial disputes, including asset-freeze orders (Mareva injunctions). They are exceptional but not categorically unavailable.
why_B_wrong: The ability to move to set aside an ex parte order does not reduce the moving party's obligations. The duty of full and frank disclosure and the urgency requirement are additional requirements beyond the standard RJR-MacDonald test.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The moving party on an ex parte motion must show urgency justifying no notice and must make full and frank disclosure of all material facts.
why_D_wrong: Ex parte injunctions are temporary orders, typically returnable within a short time (often within days) so the opposing party can be heard. They are not permanent orders.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311; Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 37.07

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty of candour to the court / ex parte disclosure obligations

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Notice of Motion — Required Contents and Notice Period
fact_pattern: Beatrix serves a notice of motion on the opposing party on a Monday. The motion is scheduled to be heard the following Wednesday, which is nine days later. The opposing party objects that insufficient notice has been given under the Rules of Civil Procedure.
call_of_question: Is the opposing party's objection well-founded?

options.A: No — a notice of motion requires only 5 clear days' notice, so 9 days is more than sufficient.
options.B: Yes — r.37.07(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure requires at least 10 days' notice of a motion; serving 9 days before the hearing date is deficient by one day.
options.C: No — the 10-day notice requirement applies only to motions for summary judgment, not ordinary motions.
options.D: Yes — the notice period for any motion is 21 days, so 9 days' notice is far too short.

explanation: Under Rule 37.07(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) provides that, unless the court orders otherwise, a notice of motion shall be served at least 10 days before the hearing date. Nine days' notice falls one day short of the mandatory 10-day minimum. The opposing party's objection is well-founded. The 10-day default applies to motions generally; it is not limited to summary judgment motions or any other specific category.
why_A_wrong: The default notice period under r.37.07(1) is 10 days, not 5 days. A 5-day period would also be deficient.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 37.07(1) requires at least 10 days' notice; 9 days is one day short and the objection is valid.
why_C_wrong: The 10-day notice requirement in r.37.07(1) applies to motions generally, not only to summary judgment motions.
why_D_wrong: The default notice period for motions is 10 days under r.37.07(1), not 21 days. A 21-day requirement does not appear for ordinary motions.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Notice of Motion — Required Contents and Notice Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.07(1)

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Motion Record — Required Contents and Filing
fact_pattern: Counsel for the moving party prepares a motion record for an upcoming motion. He includes: (1) a table of contents, (2) the notice of motion, (3) all affidavits and other evidence to be used on the motion, (4) a draft order, and (5) transcripts of any cross-examinations on affidavits. The opposing party argues that the motion record is missing a required document.
call_of_question: Which of the following must also be included in a motion record under r.37.10 of the Rules of Civil Procedure?

options.A: A copy of every pleading filed in the action to date.
options.B: The factum of the moving party setting out a concise statement of facts and law.
options.C: The trial transcript from any prior proceedings between the parties.
options.D: A certificate signed by all parties confirming they have exchanged all relevant documents.

explanation: Under Rule 37.10(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure specifies the required contents of a motion record. In addition to the table of contents, notice of motion, supporting affidavits and evidence, transcripts of cross-examinations, and draft order, the motion record must also include a copy of every pleading or other document on which the motion is based that has been filed in the proceeding. The opposing party is correct that the pleadings filed in the action must be included in the motion record.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 37.10(2) requires the motion record to include a copy of every pleading or other document on which the motion is based that has been filed.
why_B_wrong: A factum is a separate document from the motion record itself and is governed by r.37.10(6). It may or may not be required depending on the nature of the motion, but it is not part of the motion record proper.
why_C_wrong: Trial transcripts from prior proceedings are not a required component of a motion record under r.37.10. Only transcripts of cross-examinations on affidavits filed in the proceeding are required.
why_D_wrong: A certificate signed by all parties is not a required component of a motion record under the Rules. This is not a recognized procedural requirement.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Motion Record — Required Contents and Filing
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.10(2)

tested_concepts: motion record required contents under r.37.10
pleadings must be included in motion record

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Motion Without Notice — Permitted Circumstances and Disclosure Duty
fact_pattern: Sebastiano brings an urgent motion without notice (ex parte) to preserve certain electronic evidence he believes the defendant is about to destroy. On the motion, Sebastiano's counsel fails to disclose to the judge that the defendant had already voluntarily agreed in writing three days earlier to preserve all relevant electronic records pending the litigation. The court grants the order. The defendant subsequently moves to set aside the preservation order.
call_of_question: What is the most likely outcome on the defendant's motion to set aside the order?

options.A: The order will be maintained because the defendant had an obligation to bring the agreement to the court's attention before the ex parte motion was heard.
options.B: The order will be maintained unless the defendant can show it was prejudiced by the non-disclosure.
options.C: The court will set aside the order because Sebastiano's counsel breached the duty of full and frank disclosure by failing to disclose the defendant's prior agreement to preserve the evidence, which was material to whether the ex parte order was necessary.
options.D: The court will set aside the order only if the defendant can prove that Sebastiano's counsel acted in bad faith.

explanation: Under Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.07(7), On a motion without notice under r.37.07(7) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the moving party owes the court a duty of full and frank disclosure of all material facts, including facts that are adverse to the moving party's position. The defendant's prior written agreement to preserve electronic records was a material fact directly relevant to the necessity of the ex parte preservation order — had the court known of the agreement, it may well have declined to grant the order or required notice to be given. Failure to disclose a material fact on an ex parte motion is a recognised ground for setting aside the resulting order, regardless of whether the non-disclosure was intentional. The court has discretion to set aside or vary the order.
why_A_wrong: The duty of full and frank disclosure rests on the moving party, not on the absent respondent. The defendant had no obligation to inform the court of its own agreement; that was Sebastiano's counsel's responsibility.
why_B_wrong: The test for setting aside an ex parte order for non-disclosure does not require proof of actual prejudice. A material non-disclosure is itself sufficient ground to exercise the court's discretion to set aside the order.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The failure to disclose the defendant's prior preservation agreement breaches the duty of full and frank disclosure, and the order is liable to be set aside on that basis.
why_D_wrong: Bad faith is not required. The duty of full and frank disclosure is an objective standard. Even an innocent or negligent failure to disclose a material fact can ground setting aside an ex parte order.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Motion Without Notice — Permitted Circumstances and Disclosure Duty
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.37.07(7)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty of candour to the court on ex parte motion

tested_concepts: duty of full and frank disclosure on ex parte motions
material non-disclosure grounds for setting aside order

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Fernanda brings a motion to compel production of certain documents. The court grants the motion and awards costs to Fernanda. The judge does not give any special reasons for the costs award and simply says 'costs to the successful party.' The defendant asks whether this is an unusual outcome.
call_of_question: Which statement best describes the general principle governing costs on motions under the Rules of Civil Procedure?

options.A: Costs on motions are always in the cause — they are never awarded immediately after a motion and always await the outcome of the trial.
options.B: Under r.57.01, the general principle is that costs follow the event, meaning the successful party on a motion is presumptively entitled to the costs of the motion.
options.C: Costs on motions are awarded at the court's discretion but the starting presumption is that no costs are payable on interlocutory motions.
options.D: Costs on a motion can only be awarded at the end of trial; interim cost awards are not permitted.

explanation: Under r.57.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the court has a broad discretion in awarding costs, but the general principle is that costs follow the event — the successful party is presumptively entitled to its costs. On a motion, this means the party who succeeds on the motion is ordinarily awarded its costs of the motion. The court retains discretion to depart from this principle in appropriate circumstances (e.g., where the motion was necessary due to the conduct of both parties), but the default starting point is that the successful party receives its costs.
why_A_wrong: Costs 'in the cause' is one possible costs disposition on a motion, but it is not the default rule. The general principle under r.57.01 is that costs follow the event — awarded to the successful party at the conclusion of the motion.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 57.01 establishes costs follow the event as the general principle, with the successful party presumptively entitled to costs.
why_C_wrong: There is no presumption that no costs are payable on interlocutory motions. The general principle is costs follow the event under r.57.01.
why_D_wrong: Costs can be — and routinely are — awarded immediately at the conclusion of a motion. There is no rule restricting costs awards to the end of trial.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Costs on Motions — General Principles
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.57.01

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Undertaking as to Damages — Condition for Interlocutory Injunction
fact_pattern: Patel Distribution Ltd. obtains an interlocutory injunction restraining Kowalski Logistics Inc. from operating a competing business pending trial. The trial judge ultimately finds in favour of Kowalski and dismisses Patel's action. Kowalski suffered significant revenue losses during the period the injunction was in force.
call_of_question: What is the standard condition imposed on a moving party who obtains an interlocutory injunction, and what is its significance to Kowalski?

options.A: There is no standard condition on injunctions; Kowalski has no remedy for losses suffered during the injunction period.
options.B: The moving party is routinely required to give an undertaking as to damages — a promise to pay any losses suffered by the restrained party if the injunction is ultimately found to have been wrongly granted; Kowalski may claim on this undertaking.
options.C: An undertaking as to damages is optional and imposed only at the court's discretion; it is rare in commercial disputes.
options.D: Kowalski may recover losses only by commencing a new action for wrongful injunction, not by enforcing any undertaking given on the original motion.

explanation: Under RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311, As a standard condition of granting an interlocutory injunction in Ontario, the moving party is required to give an undertaking as to damages. This undertaking is a promise to compensate the restrained party for any losses it suffers as a result of the injunction if the injunction is ultimately found to have been wrongly granted — that is, if the moving party does not succeed at trial. The undertaking protects the restrained party against the risk of being wrongly enjoined. Once Patel loses at trial, Kowalski may pursue the undertaking as to damages to recover its losses caused by the wrongful injunction, without commencing a separate action.
why_A_wrong: The undertaking as to damages is a standard condition of most interlocutory injunctions. Kowalski has a remedy through the undertaking and is not left without recourse.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The undertaking as to damages is a standard condition on interlocutory injunctions; Kowalski may claim on it after the action is dismissed in its favour.
why_C_wrong: An undertaking as to damages is a standard, routinely required condition — not an exceptional or optional one. Courts impose it as a matter of course when granting interlocutory injunctions.
why_D_wrong: Kowalski does not need to commence a new action. The undertaking as to damages, given on the original motion, is enforceable within the existing proceeding. A separate wrongful injunction action is not necessary.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Undertaking as to Damages — Condition for Interlocutory Injunction
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.40

tested_concepts: undertaking as to damages — standard condition on injunctions
moving party's obligation if injunction wrongly granted
claim on undertaking after trial loss

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Striking Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action Under r.21.01
fact_pattern: The defendant brings a motion under r.21.01(1)(b) to strike the plaintiff's statement of claim on the ground that it discloses no reasonable cause of action. The plaintiff's claim alleges that the defendant, a large retailer, deliberately created a shopping atmosphere that was unconscionably pressuring. The defendant argues the claim is novel and has no legal basis recognized in Ontario.
call_of_question: Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure and Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959, what is the test for striking a pleading on the ground that it discloses no reasonable cause of action?

options.A: The court strikes the claim if the defendant can demonstrate that the plaintiff is unlikely to succeed at trial on a balance of probabilities.
options.B: The court strikes the claim only where it is plain and obvious, assuming the facts as pleaded are true, that the claim discloses no reasonable cause of action; a novel or uncertain claim should not be struck on this ground.
options.C: The court strikes the claim if any element of the pleaded claim is disputed by the defendant on the facts.
options.D: The court strikes the claim where the allegations in the statement of claim are improbable or speculative.

explanation: Under r.21.01(1)(b) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), the court may strike a pleading that discloses no reasonable cause of action. The test, established by the Supreme Court of Canada in Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959, is whether it is plain and obvious — assuming the facts as pleaded to be true — that the claim discloses no reasonable cause of action. The threshold is high: the claim should not be struck merely because it is novel, because the law is uncertain, or because the claim may be difficult to establish. Only if there is no chance of success should the claim be struck at this stage. On a motion under r.21.01(1)(b), no evidence is admissible — only the pleadings are considered.
why_A_wrong: The test is not a probability of success assessment. The court does not weigh evidence or assess likelihood of success under r.21.01(1)(b). It asks only whether it is plain and obvious the claim cannot succeed.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Hunt v Carey plain and obvious test requires assuming facts as pleaded are true; novel claims are not struck simply because the law is uncertain.
why_C_wrong: Disputed facts are irrelevant on a r.21.01(1)(b) motion. The court assumes all pleaded facts to be true. The defendant cannot raise factual disputes to strike the pleading.
why_D_wrong: Improbability or speculativeness of the allegations is not the test. The court assumes the facts as pleaded are true and asks only whether the claim is plain and obviously unsustainable in law.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Striking Pleadings — No Reasonable Cause of Action Under r.21.01
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.21.01(1)(b); Hunt v Carey Canada Inc, [1990] 2 SCR 959

tested_concepts: plain and obvious test — Hunt v Carey Canada Inc [1990] 2 SCR 959
no evidence admissible on r.21.01 motion
